title: Worked Example - Absolute vs Relative Paths

content.problemStatement:
This example shows you how an **absolute path** links to a different website and a **relative path** links to a file on the same site.

---

**When you run the code, you'll see:**

- **Two links** on the page, each with a heading describing its type.
- Clicking the **"Visit Wikipedia"** link will take you to the Wikipedia website because it uses a full, absolute path.
- Clicking the "Learn About Us" link will open a new page because we've created a file named about.html in the same directory. This is the key lesson: a relative path works when the file exists.

---

**Try it yourself:**
1. **Change the absolute path:**  
   Edit the `href` of the Wikipedia link to a different external site, like `https://www.nasa.gov`, to see how easy it is to change the destination.

2. **Practice with a relative path:**  
   Add another link that points to a file, such as `Contact Us`. To make this new link work, you must create a file named `contact.html` in the same folder. This new file will contain the contact information, behaving the same way as your "About Us" link.

Click **Run** to see the two different types of links in action.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
